binding_constraint_top1: A shared user-settings cache architecture whose invalidation / rewrite behavior could trigger system-wide write amplification and replication delays across many dependent services.
binding_constraint_top3: Centralized cache-write path with unsafe blast radius
Lack of isolation around a high-fan-out user-settings caching mechanism
Architecture that allows cache recalculation and rewrite storms to cascade into core services
slow_variables: Architectural decoupling / isolation of the cache mechanism
Protection mechanisms around mass invalidation and rewrite events
Monitoring and killswitch maturity for shared infrastructure components
Resilience of downstream services to replication lag from shared-state overload
contributing_factors: A bug caused every user's cache to expire, get recalculated, and get rewritten
The increased load caused replication delays that cascaded to affected services
The same underlying cause had already appeared in a prior incident
Many product surfaces depended on the stressed mechanism
good_interventions_ranked: Isolate the user-settings cache mechanism on dedicated infrastructure or partitioned fault domains so failures do not cascade broadly.
Add guardrails against mass expiry / rewrite events, including staged invalidation, quotas, and rollbackable killswitches.
Instrument leading indicators on cache write amplification and replication delay, with automatic abort thresholds during risky deployments.
bad_interventions: Optimizing user-facing retry messaging while leaving the write-storm path intact
Treating this as only a deployment bug rather than an architectural blast-radius problem
Adding more frontend failover without fixing the shared cache dependency
early_warning_signals: Sudden spike in cache expirations, recalculations, or rewrite volume per user
Replication lag crossing predefined thresholds during or immediately after deployment
Cross-service error increases tied to shared user-settings reads/writes
Repeat incidents linked to the same mechanism
distractors_to_penalize: Surface-level SRE playbook updates without architecture change
Focusing on Copilot or API symptoms separately rather than the shared dependency
evidence: GitHub reports roughly 40% github.com request failures and 43% API failures during the incident.
The report says the same underlying cause had appeared in an early-February incident.
A bug caused every user's cache to expire, get recalculated, and get rewritten.
The increased load caused replication delays that cascaded to affected services.
GitHub says it added a killswitch, improved monitoring, and is moving the cache mechanism to a dedicated host.
